description: The provided image is an irrelevant functional icon displaying the text "Check for updates", with a blue-yellow segmented circular ring and a red bookmark-style graphic inside the ring. This image has no matching relationship with the research content of the target paper focusing on the novel Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex low-density steel, so no valid material science related information such as microstructure characterization, mechanical property data, processing route or phase structure can be extracted from this mismatched image.

This figure characterizes the microstructural and phase evolution of the novel Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex low-density steel after cold rolling (CR) followed by annealing at 600–1050 °C for 5 min. Subplot (a) shows XRD patterns with diffraction angle 2θ ranging from 35° to 80° for CR state and samples annealed at 600 °C, 700 °C, 800 °C, 900 °C, 950 °C, 1000 °C, 1050 °C, identifying FCC austenite, BCC ferrite, Cr₇C₃ carbide, κ-carbide, and β phase, revealing that β phase vanishes at higher annealing temperatures, Cr₇C₃ and κ-carbide form at 950–1000 °C, and all carbides dissolve at 1050 °C. Subplot (b) presents TEM results for DP950-5 (annealed at 950 °C for 5 min): bright-field image, Cr EDS mapping, and SAED pattern confirming grain boundary precipitates are Cr₇C₃. Subplot (c) shows TEM characterization for DP1000-5 (annealed at 1000 °C for 5 min): bright-field images at different magnifications, SAED patterns of ferrite and austenite containing intragranular nano κ-carbides with size of 0.86±0.20 nm and volume fraction of 1.56%. It verifies that annealing temperature regulates phase composition and precipitation behavior, and 1000 °C annealing introduces beneficial intragranular nano κ-carbides without harmful coarse grain boundary carbides, contributing to excellent strength-ductility synergy of the steel.

This figure presents Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of microstructures for a novel Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex low-density steel after different cold-rolling and annealing processes. The top row (a₁-f₁) are phase (PH) maps: cyan regions represent face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite, red regions represent body-centered cubic (BCC) ferrite, with a uniform scale bar of 20 μm. The tested samples include cold-rolled (CR) steel, DP950-1 (annealed at 950℃ for 1 min), DP950-5 (annealed at 950℃ for 5 min), DP1000-1 (annealed at 1000℃ for 1 min), DP1000-5 (annealed at 1000℃ for 5 min), DP1050-1 (annealed at 1050℃ for 1 min) from left to right. The bottom row (a₂-f₂) are grain-reference-orientation-deviation (GROD) maps of austenite: yellow denotes 0°~2° orientation deviation (recrystallized grains), warm tones denote 2°~180° deviation (deformed grains), and dark lines are high-angle grain boundaries (>15° misorientation). Observed trends: CR and 950℃ annealed samples contain massive shear bands and incompletely recrystallized austenite, with the highest ferrite fraction in DP950-5; as annealing temperature rises to 1000℃ and above, austenite fully recrystallizes, ferrite fraction decreases, ferrite bands become thinner, annealing twins appear, and austenite grains grow with longer annealing duration. This figure confirms that annealing temperature and holding time regulate recrystallization behavior, phase composition, and microstructure morphology, laying the microstructure foundation for the excellent combination of strength and ductility of the developed duplex steel.

This figure contains two subplots demonstrating mechanical properties of the novel designed duplex low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr steel. Subplot (a) presents engineering stress-strain curves of specimens subjected to different annealing processes, including DP950-1 (950℃ annealed for 1min), DP950-5 (950℃ for 5min), DP1000-1 (1000℃ for 1min), DP1000-5 (1000℃ for 5min, tested along rolling direction), DP1000-5-TD (1000℃ for 5min, tested along transverse direction) and DP1050-1 (1050℃ for 1min), with engineering strain (0-50%) as x-axis and engineering stress (0-1500 MPa) as y-axis. Subplot (b) compares specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS, 80-260 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³) versus total elongation (TE, 0-60%) of the developed steels (marked as red stars) with other high-strength metallic materials (2000 series Al alloy, Ti6Al4V, austenitic steels, duplex steels), with contour lines indicating SUTS×TE values from 2 to 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%. The developed steels, especially DP1000-5 and DP1050-1, exhibit unprecedented combination of specific tensile strength and ductility, with their SUTS×TE product exceeding 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%, far superior to existing reference metallic materials.

This figure presents SEM micrographs (a₁, b₁) and corresponding EBSD phase distribution maps (a₂, b₂) of typical tensile cracks in two Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex low-density steel samples: DP1000-5 (annealed at 1000℃ for 5 min, tensile tested along rolling direction RD, group a) and DP1000-5-TD (same annealing process, tensile tested along transverse direction TD, group b). Light blue in EBSD maps denotes FCC austenite, red denotes BCC ferrite. For DP1000-5, cracks nucleate at α/γ interfaces, propagate along normal direction ND, and deflect when encountering BCC δ-ferrite bands parallel to RD, which hinders rapid crack propagation and delivers high ductility. For DP1000-5-TD, δ-ferrite/austenite interfaces are perpendicular to tensile direction, so cracks nucleate at these interfaces and quickly connect across sample thickness, leading to much lower ductility. Scale bars are 20 μm for group a and 50 μm for group b. This figure confirms that δ-ferrite band orientation dominates the tensile ductility anisotropy of the steel.
